summary: react-cleaner fetches and evaluates remote JavaScript from jsonkeeper.com on import while staging environment variables for exfiltration
details: On require() of this package, lib/writer.js performs an axios GET to https://www.jsonkeeper.com/b/MYUKZ and passes the response content directly to eval(), executing arbitrary attacker-controlled JavaScript fetched from a public mutable paste host. The same module assembles a `data` object containing the spread of process.env plus os.platform(), os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, and the host's non-internal MAC addresses — a staging buffer the eval'd second stage can reference to exfiltrate installer secrets (CI tokens, AWS keys, npm tokens, etc.). A hex-encoded fallback URL (https://www.jsonkeeper.com/b/HY6M6) and obfuscated method names ('axios','get','then') are constructed via a custom decoder, providing a parallel dropper channel. The package masquerades as the legitimate pino logger: package.json declares `"main":"pino.js"`, `"homepage":"https://getpino.io"`, `"keywords":["fast","logger","stream","json"]`, and bundles pino-branded images, while the package name `react-cleaner` is unrelated to its self-described purpose. Identity-impersonation + import-time remote eval + bulk environment enumeration are three independent block signals.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
